title: Patching and colour-matching a painted wall
subtitle: Repair a small area of damaged plaster or paint and blend it into the existing finish
excerpt: How to fill, prime, and repaint a small damaged area on a painted wall so the repair is invisible. Covers why fresh paint on a patch always looks different, how to feather the repair, and when to consider painting the full wall.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6150: Code of practice for painting of buildings. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A patch of dull, flat paint visible on a wall where a repair has been made and the repaired area has a different porosity from the surrounding surface. The fresh filler or bare plaster absorbs more paint than the old, sealed surface, leaving a visibly different sheen even when the same colour is used. Resolved by sealing the repair with a diluted PVA or primer before painting.

aliases: painting over filled hole in wall

content.text: A patch on a painted wall almost always looks different from the surrounding surface when it dries, even when you use the same tin of paint. Fresh paint on a sealed old surface looks more opaque. Fresh paint on a filler or bare plaster patch looks duller because the filler absorbs more paint; a

. The fade in old paint also means that even the same tin of paint laid next to it may look brighter. Understanding these factors helps you manage expectations: a small patch can often be made invisible; a large patch or very old paint almost always warrants repainting the full wall.

Lightweight one-coat filler. Sandpaper (120-grit and 180-grit). A 50 mm filling knife. A 25 mm brush or a 100 mm mini roller for the paint. PVA adhesive diluted 1 part to 5 parts water, or a proprietary plaster primer. The original paint (or a freshly mixed colour match from a paint retailer). Masking tape (optional, for very large patches).

Apply lightweight filler to the damaged area with the filling knife, pressing it firmly into the hole or crack. Overfill slightly so the filler stands proud of the surface. Allow to dry. Sand flat with 120-grit first, then finish with 180-grit, feathering the edges of the filler out 50 mm onto the surrounding paint. The feathered edge blends the patch into the wall; a sharp edge creates a visible halo under paint.

Apply a thin coat of diluted PVA (1 part PVA to 5 parts water) or a plaster primer to the sanded patch only. Do not apply it to the surrounding wall. Allow to dry completely. This seals the filler's surface porosity so it accepts paint at the same rate as the old sealed wall beside it; eliminating the suction shadow.
Step 3, apply paint in thin overlapping coats

Apply the first coat of paint to the patch, extending it 50 to 100 mm beyond the patch edge onto the surrounding wall. Use a brush rather than a roller if the patch is small; a roller can create texture differences visible in raking light. Apply in a light cross-hatch motion, blending the edge into the existing finish while the paint is still wet. Allow to dry. The first coat will often look patchy; this is normal. Apply the second coat in the same way. In most cases two thin coats over a sealed patch produce a visually acceptable result.
When to paint the full wall

Repaint the full wall when: the existing paint is more than 5 years old and has faded; the colour match is off despite using the original tin; or the patch is wider than 30 cm. A full repaint from corner to corner always looks better than a visible patch. It takes only 30 to 60 minutes more than a large patch job.

attrs.symptom: Patch is visible as a dull circle even after two coats
attrs.cause: Filler was not sealed before painting; suction shadow is showing.
attrs.fix: Apply a coat of diluted PVA to the dull area, allow to dry fully, and apply a further coat of paint.

attrs.symptom: Colour of the patch looks different even though the same tin was used
attrs.cause: Old paint has faded or the tin was mixed to a slightly different batch than the original.
attrs.fix: Feather the patch as far as possible into the surrounding wall while wet. If the difference is still noticeable when dry, repaint the full wall.
